narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an upper-middle-class family background, you've put in the effort to pursue law school, including planning to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 41.9% chance of failure for you—high enough that your application was rejected to protect the school's outcomes. This decision hinged on a few key aspects of your profile, compared to typical students in our dataset. Your undergraduate GPA, which is right around the average of 3.21, actually worked strongly in your favor, suggesting to the model that you have the academic foundation to succeed on the bar. Similarly, your upper-middle family income stood out positively, as it nudged the prediction toward passing, likely reflecting resources that support strong performance. On the flip side, your Year 1 school decile of 1—much lower than the typical 5.71—pushed the model toward expecting failure, indicating early struggles relative to peers. Your LSAT score of 26.50, well below the average 36.70, also contributed to that concern, as lower scores often signal challenges ahead. Your Year 3 decile of 2, again below the average 5.53, added to this influence, though less strongly. Being full-time, like most applicants at 92.7%, provided a small positive boost. Overall, while your GPA and background helped balance things, your lower deciles and LSAT were the top factors tipping the scale toward predicted failure compared to stronger profiles that get approved. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your early-year performance through targeted prep, and reapplying with those improvements—many students in similar situations succeed on their next try. We're rooting for you to turn this around.
